title: Mobile Device Use While Crossing the Street: Utilizing the Theory of Planned Behavior

sections.name: Introduction
sections.summary: Starts from public-safety stakes with concrete fatality/injury/cost statistics, then narrows to distracted pedestrian behavior and motivates theory-driven analysis.
sections.organization_pattern: macro safety problem -> trend statistics -> specific behavioral risk -> prior evidence ambiguity -> present analytical focus
sections.citation_pattern: dense citation use for factual claims; each numerical or contextual claim is anchored
useful_phrases: Additionally, a concerning trend is illustrated by...
The present study was designed to...

sections.name: Discussion
sections.summary: Interprets results by comparing them against prior studies, explains why differences may arise, and links findings back to theory and measurement choices.
sections.organization_pattern: main result -> contrast with prior studies -> explanation of divergence -> theory linkage -> implication
sections.citation_pattern: comparative citation use; prior studies are introduced to support similarity/contrast rather than mere listing
useful_phrases: This is in contrast to... yet similar to...

global_patterns.opening_strategy: use concrete stakes and numbers before narrowing to focal behavior
global_patterns.synthesis_strategy: compare new evidence against specific prior findings and explain divergence
global_patterns.comparison_strategy: contrast predictors, populations, and measurement framing
global_patterns.gap_strategy: highlight ambiguity or inconsistent evidence before defining need
global_patterns.ending_strategy: translate findings into intervention or research implications

notes: Not a review paper, but a strong prose reference for evidence-grounded academic writing in pedestrian behavior research.
